18: de estin sophia ho echon noun psephisato ton arithmon tou theriou arithmos gar anthropou estin kai ho arithmos autou hexakosioi hexekonta hex: '666' — the number of the beast. Arithmos anthropou (number of a human/humanity) — the beast's number is not cosmic but human, not transcendent but sub-divine. Gematria (assigning numerical values to letters) was a common Greco-Roman literary technique. The best-attested solution: NERON KAISER in Hebrew letters (nun=50 + resh=200 + vav=6 + nun=50 + qoph=100 + samek=60 + resh=200 = 666). Nero Caesar as the prototype of the beast — the first Roman persecutor of Christians — makes the political-historical reference legible to John's seven churches (ca. 90-95 CE, under Domitian, the 'second Nero').
1: ek tēs thalassēs: from the sea. The sea (thalassa) as the source of evil and chaos is the OT background (Dan 7:3 — four great beasts come from the sea; Isa 57:20 — the wicked are like the tossing sea; Ps 74:13 — God crushing the sea-monster). Daniel's four beasts (lion, bear, leopard, terrible beast) represent successive empires; Revelation's single beast combines all four into the ultimate totalizing empire. Ten horns and seven heads with ten diadems: the beast's crown (diadema = royal crown, symbol of authority) count exceeds the dragon's seven (12:3), signaling that the beast surpasses the dragon in earthly political power.
2: to thērion ho eidon ēn homoion pardali: the beast combines leopard, bear, and lion — precisely the animals of Dan 7:3-7 but in reverse order (Daniel: lion → bear → leopard → terrible beast; Revelation lists them in reverse). The reversal may be chronological: Revelation's beast is the final empire, incorporating all previous empires into itself. Ho drakōn edōken autō tēn dynamin autou kai ton thronon autou kai exousian megalēn: the dragon gave it his power, throne, and great authority. The beast's imperial power is explicitly derived — it is not autonomous but delegated from the dragon, who himself has only usurped power that belongs to God.
3: hōs esphagmenēn eis thanaton: as if slaughtered to death — esphagmenēn is the same perfect passive participle used of the Lamb in 5:6 (hōs esphagmenon). The beast parodies the Lamb: slain-and-healed vs. slain-and-risen. The deadly wound healed (hē plēgē tou thanatou autou etherapeuthe) inverts the resurrection: the Lamb was killed and lives (1:18); the beast appears killed and revives. The Nero redivivus legend (Nero would return from death to reclaim power) likely underlies this imagery — Domitian as the second Nero, the beast revived. Hē holē gē ethaumasen opisō tou thēriou: the whole earth marveled and followed (opisō, behind, in the wake of) the beast — wonder as the basis for worship.
4: Tis homoios tō thēriō kai tis dynatai polemēsai met autou: "Who is like the beast, and who can make war against him?" — a deliberate parody of the divine acclamation in Exod 15:11 (Who is like you among the gods, O LORD?) and Ps 89:6 (Who is like the LORD?). The beast's apparent invulnerability generates a pseudo-monotheism: if no one can defeat him, he must be divine. The Roman imperial cult operated precisely on this logic — Caesar's universal power as evidence of divine status. Revelation unmasks this as dragon-worship (tō drakōnti prosekunēsan).
5: stoma laloun megala kai blasphēmias: a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies — the exact description of Daniel's little horn in Dan 7:8, 11, 20. The beast is identified as the Daniel-beast. Edothē autō exousia poiēsai mēnas tesserakonta [kai] dyo: authority was given (edothē — divine passive throughout ch. 13) for 42 months. The divine passive (edothē = it was given by God) is theologically critical: even the beast's authority is divinely permitted, not self-generated. God governs even the dragon's agents. 42 months = the same period as 12:6, 14 — the whole era of the beast's dominion is within God's sovereign time-frame.
6: blasphēmein to onoma autou kai tēn skēnēn autou: to blaspheme his name and his dwelling (skēnē). In Roman imperial theology, Domitian's title Dominus et Deus (Lord and God) directly appropriated the divine name. The blasphemy against God's skēnē (tabernacle/dwelling) and those who dwell in heaven targets the whole heavenly order: both the divine presence and the heavenly community. The beast's speech is the inversion of the Trisagion (4:8): where the heavenly creatures cry "Holy, holy, holy," the beast blasphemes.
7: edothē autō poiēsai polemon meta tōn hagiōn kai nikēsai autous: it was given to him (edothē — divine passive again) to make war on the saints and conquer (nikēsai) them. The apparent paradox: the beast conquers the saints, yet the saints conquer the beast (12:11). The resolution: the beast conquers physically (martyrdom) while the saints conquer spiritually (faith through death). The divine passive confirms that even the beast's persecution of the saints is within God's sovereign permission — a pastoral word to persecuted believers. Edothē autō exousia epi pasan phylēn kai laon kai glōssan kai ethnos: universal authority — the fourfold formula (tribe, people, tongue, nation) that in Revelation marks the scope of either the gospel (14:6) or the beast's dominion.
8: hou ou gegraptai to onoma autou en tō bibliō tēs zōēs tou Arniou tou esphagmenou apo katabolēs kosmou: everyone will worship whose name was not written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world. The phrase "from the foundation of the world" is grammatically ambiguous: it can modify "slain" (the Lamb's sacrifice is eternal in God's plan, before creation) or "written" (election before creation). Both readings are theologically defensible and both are affirmed in Scripture (1 Pet 1:19-20 supports the former; Eph 1:4 and Rev 17:8 support the latter). The book of life belongs to the Lamb: election and atonement are inseparable — those written are those covered by the slain Lamb's blood.
9: ei tis echei ous akousatō: "If anyone has an ear, let him hear." The verbatim refrain of the seven letters (2:7, 11, 17, 29; 3:6, 13, 22) — each letter ends with this call to attend to the Spirit's word. Its insertion here signals that the vision of the beast is not mere symbolic spectacle but a pastoral word addressed to the churches. What the Seer sees in vision is what the churches experience historically: persecution, economic pressure, the demand for beast-cult worship. The call to hear is a call to discern their own situation in the prophetic vision.
10: ei tis eis aichmalōsian eis aichmalōsian hypagei: if anyone is for captivity, into captivity he goes. The saying mirrors Jer 15:2 (those for captivity, to captivity; those for the sword, to the sword). The application in context: those appointed for tribulation must endure it (hōde estin hē hypomone kai hē pistis tōn hagiōn = here is the patient endurance and faith of the saints). Hypomone (patient endurance, steadfast perseverance) is the key virtue of the Apocalypse — appearing at 1:9; 2:2-3, 19; 3:10; 13:10; 14:12. It is not passive resignation but active faithfulness maintained under eschatological pressure.
11: allo thērion ek tēs gēs: another beast, from the earth (not the sea). The second beast (later called the false prophet: 16:13; 19:20; 20:10). Dyo kerata homoia arniō (two horns like a lamb): a wolf in lamb's clothing — its appearance mimics the Lamb of ch. 5, but elalei hōs drakōn (it spoke like a dragon). The mouth reveals the nature: Jesus warned that false prophets come in sheep's clothing (Matt 7:15). The false prophet's two horns may evoke prophetic authority (horns = power); its lamb-like appearance = religious legitimacy.
12: poiei tēn gēn kai tous en autē katoikountas hina proskunēsousin to thērion to prōton: it causes the earth to worship the first beast. The false prophet's function is entirely doxological — it redirects worship. This is the demonic inversion of the Holy Spirit's role, who glorifies Christ (John 16:14): the false prophet glorifies the beast. Hē plēgē tou thanatou autou etherapeuthe: the healed death-wound again — the false prophet uses this miracle as the basis for coercion to worship.
13: semeia megala: great signs (sēmeia). The false prophet makes fire come down from heaven (ek tou ouranou katababainein) in front of everyone — echoing Elijah calling down fire on the prophets of Baal (1 Kgs 18:38) and on soldiers of Ahaziah (2 Kgs 1:10-12). The false prophet mimics the greatest OT prophetic miracle to legitimize the beast-cult. Jesus warned that false christs and false prophets will perform great signs and wonders to deceive even the elect (Matt 24:24; Mark 13:22) — Revelation shows this happening with the beast's prophet.
14: plana tous katoikountas epi tēs gēs dia ta sēmeia: it deceives (planaō — leads astray) those who dwell on the earth by means of the signs. Planaō is the core deception verb in Revelation — the dragon is "the one who leads the whole world astray" (12:9). The false prophet extends this deception through miraculous authentication. Legōn tois katoikousin epi tēs gēs poiēsai eikona tō thēriō: telling those who dwell on the earth to make an eikōn (image) of the beast. Roman emperor worship included statues of the emperor receiving divine honors — the eikōn is the cultus statue that becomes the focus of imperial worship.
15: dounai pneuma tē eikoni tou thēriou: to give pneuma (breath/spirit) to the image. Pneuma here = breath or life-spirit. The false prophet animates the idol — whether through ventriloquism, priestly manipulation, or demonic agency. This action parodies God breathing life into Adam (Gen 2:7: enephysēsen eis to prosōpon autou pnoēn zōēs). The idolatrous image receives a counterfeit pneuma from the false prophet rather than the life-breath that the true God alone can give. Hina hosoi ean mē proskunēsōsin tē eikoni tou thēriou apokanthōsin: those who refuse to worship the image are killed — the ultimate expression of the beast-cult's totalitarian claim.
16: poiē pantas: it causes all — the fourfold social spectrum (small and great, rich and poor, free and slave) is exhaustive: no social category is exempt from the economic-religious pressure. Charagma (mark, brand) — the technical Greek term for an official stamp, seal, or brand used on imperial documents, coins, and animals/slaves. The imperial seal on commercial documents and the use of coins bearing the emperor's image (with divine titles) made economic participation in Roman society a constant low-grade act of emperor acknowledgment. Revelation's charagma intensifies this to a formal religious mark — the satanic parody of the seal of God on the foreheads of the 144,000 (7:3; 9:4; 14:1).
17: hina mē tis dynētai agorasai ē pōlēsai ei mē ho echōn to charagma: that no one can buy (agorasai) or sell (pōlēsai) without the mark. Economic exclusion as persecution — the beast's economic control makes daily survival contingent on beast-cult participation. The verb agorazō (to buy) is theologically loaded in Revelation: it is also the redemption verb — the Lamb purchased (ēgorasas) the saints (5:9; 14:3-4). Those who refuse to be economically "bought" by the beast have been already "bought" by the Lamb. The two purchases are mutually exclusive.
